Management of Bleeding Gastric Varices: A Practical Approach to an Impractical Problem
  • Source: American Journal of Gastroenterology, Vol. XX
  • Topic: Management of Bleeding Gastric Varices (GV)
  • Author(s): Henry Z, Suresh D, Cardenas A
Overview of Gastric Variceal Bleeding
  • GV bleeding --> uncommon but potentially deadly complication of portal hypertension
  • Rarer than esophageal varices (EV) bleeding
  • Often more severe with higher mortality and ↑ requirement for blood transfusion
  • Management --> difficult due to myriad of pathophysiological variants
Key Pathophysiological Features
  • Some GV develop with comorbid esophageal varices
  • At least 50% present with low or normal HVPG <12 mm Hg
  • Up to 85% have portosystemic shunts associated
  • Some associated with portal or splenic vein thromboses
Challenges in Management
  • Rare occurrence --> standardized management poorly defined
  • Most recommendations based on expert interpretation of low-quality evidence
Initial Endoscopy Management
General Principles
  • Same initial steps as any suspected portal hypertensive bleed
  • GV may bleed at HVPG <12 mm Hg
  • Benefit to acutely lowering HVPG until definitive therapy completed
Clearing the Fundus
  • Must clear large pool of blood and clot in fundus completely with aspiration
  • Blood products obscure posterior wall and greater curvature --> most common bleeding locations
  • Must clear fundus even if high-risk stigmata present in esophagus
Why Clearing Fundus is Critical
  • Prevents inappropriate treatments based on false assumptions
  • Prevents complications from band ligation without clearing fundus
  • Band ligation for suspected EV bleeding without clearing fundus --> complicates repeat endoscopy
Identifying Bleeding Source
  • Active bleeding allows diagnosis of source
  • In absence of active bleeding --> evaluate for high-risk marks on GV
High-Risk Stigmata Characteristics
  • EV bleeding: typically represented by heaped-up platelet plug
  • GV bleeding: often have a flat white or clear area instead representing the bleeding diathesis
  • Sometimes --> no clear stigmata of bleeding
Distinguishing EV vs GV Bleeding
  • When both EV and GV present --> difficult to distinguish source
  • Key point: bleeding EV are much more common than bleeding GV
  • If question on source --> treating suspected bleeding EV first is reasonable
Gastric Varices Classification (Sarin Classification)
GOV1 - Gastroesophageal Varices Type 1
  • Lesser curve GV extending toward gastric body
  • Inflow from left gastric vein
  • Develop in communication with esophageal varices
GOV2 - Gastroesophageal Varices Type 2
  • Cardiofundal GV along greater curvature or posterior wall
  • Less likely anterior wall
IGV1 - Isolated Gastric Varices Type 1
  • Cardiofundal location
  • Not connected to esophageal varices
IGV2 - Isolated Gastric Varices Type 2
  • Distal GV in gastric body or antrum
  • Rare presentation
  • Most often seen with splenic vein thromboses
  • Often arise outside setting of cirrhosis or liver disease
Simplified Management Categories

1. Lesser curve GV (GOV1)

2. Cardiofundal GV (GOV2/IGV1 combined)

3. Distal GV (IGV2) --> not discussed in this review

Acute Therapy by GV Type
Lesser Curve GV Management
  • Managed similarly to EV bleeding with band ligation
  • Start a few centimeters below GE junction along lesser curve
  • Move proximally into lower third of esophagus
Cardiofundal GV Acute Management Challenges
  • Most American gastroenterologists not familiar with ECI technique
  • Reasons: lack of FDA-approved cyanoacrylate preparation + lack of training
Cardiofundal GV - NOT Recommended Therapies
  • Band ligation --> severe life-threatening bleeding when these areas ulcerate short time later
  • Alcohol-based sclerosants --> same risk of severe ulceration and rebleeding
Cardiofundal GV - Alternative Acute Options (Limited Data)
  • Clip placement
  • Thrombin injection
  • Endoloop placement
  • Hemostatic spray
  • Short-term durability --> questionable
Cardiofundal GV - Recommended Acute Therapy
  • Balloon tamponade --> safest acute mechanical intervention
  • Placement can be difficult due to lack of training
  • Endoscopic methods for balloon placement in stomach may provide more reliable confirmation
Cardiofundal GV - Preferred Acute Therapy
  • Per international guidelines --> endoscopic cyanoacrylate injection (ECI) to stop acute bleed
  • Often not available due to expertise limitations
After Initial Endoscopy - Definitive Therapy Planning
Immediate Next Steps
  • Acute bleed --> temporized with initial endoscopy
  • Goal: complete definitive therapy to prevent longer-term rebleeding
Cross-Sectional Imaging Requirement
  • Obtain contrasted cross-sectional imaging with CT or MRI
  • Purpose: define underlying vascular anatomy of varix
  • Use validated classification system (Kiyosue or Saad-Caldwell classifications)
Imaging Classification Benefits
  • Standardizes assessment of GV
  • Guides therapeutic decision making
  • Main priorities: identifying portosystemic shunt and/or portal-splenic venous thromboses
Multi-Disciplinary Approach
  • Definitive therapy often requires interventional radiology and/or endoscopic techniques
  • Important to have multi-disciplinary discussions about available treatment options
Transfer Considerations
  • If definitive therapy options not available at center --> transfer to tertiary care center once stabilized
Definitive Therapy Options by GV Type
Lesser Curve GV - Definitive Management
  • Often completed after initial endoscopy with band ligation (similar to EV)
  • Patient should start secondary prophylaxis with beta blocker (e.g., carvedilol)
  • Repeat upper endoscopy within 4 weeks of initial presentation
  • Consider pre-emptive TIPS placement per current EV management guidelines
Cardiofundal GV - Definitive Management Options
  • Endoscopic therapy: cyanoacrylate injection (ECI)
  • Endovascular therapies:
  • TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt)
  • RTO (retrograde transvenous obliteration) variants:
  • Balloon occlusion
  • Coil occlusion
  • Vascular plug occlusion
Decision-Making Factors
  • Advantages and side effects to all options (see Table 1)
  • Influenced by presence/absence of dominant shunt
  • Influenced by local expertise
Comparison of Definitive Therapies
RTO vs ECI (Randomized Controlled Trial)
  • RTO superior to prevent short and long-term rebleeding
  • Similar safety profile
RTO vs TIPS (Meta-analyses)
  • RTO superior to ECI for cardiofundal GV
  • Likely due to presence of portosystemic shunt
  • Studies primarily cohort studies
  • One RCT included --> biased through selection procedures
TIPS vs ECI Comparison by GV Type
  • Lesser curve GV (GOV1): TIPS superior over ECI
  • Cardiofundal GV (primarily GOV2): equivalent results
  • Longer-term data --> increased morbidity with TIPS
ECI Technique Recommendations
  • No standardized technique for ECI
  • Many opinions exist regarding optimal approach
  • Do NOT recommend using additives like lipiodol --> ↑ risk of systemic embolization
  • Recommend 2 mL aliquots of n-butyl-2-cyanoacrylate with repeated injections
  • Inject at different sites of varix to fill entire varix
EUS-Guided ECI
  • Published in case reports and small series
  • May provide improved results
  • Occlusion of vessel --> directly visualized under doppler ultrasound
  • Heterogeneity of studies significantly limits interpretation
TIPS with Direct Embolization
  • When no portosystemic shunt present --> TIPS may be used as definitive therapy
  • Direct embolization of varix should also be completed
  • Non-controlled data suggest this may be superior to TIPS alone
Pre-emptive TIPS After ECI (Recent RCT)
  • Recent open-label RCT suggests:
  • After initial ECI therapy for cardiofundal GV bleeding
  • Pre-emptive TIPS superior over ECI alone
  • For 1-year probability of being free from death or rebleeding
  • No long-term increase in morbidity
Imaging Findings Impact on Treatment Choice
Portosystemic Shunt Assessment
  • Most commonly gastro-renal shunt
  • Inflow from splenic hilum/splenic vein
  • Outflow through GV into left renal vein
  • Required to complete RTO procedure
Systemic Thromboembolism Risk with ECI
  • Portosystemic shunt required for systemic thromboembolism
  • Theoretical ↑ risk with ECI
  • Published literature suggests risk --> 0-2%
Hepatic Encephalopathy Risk
  • TIPS creates additional portosystemic shunt --> ↑ risk of HE
Portal Vein Thrombosis Impact
  • Severity and chronicity may complicate TIPS placement
  • Occlusive PVT --> limits access to portal system for TIPS completion
  • Chronic PVT with cavernous transformation --> typically absolute contraindication
Splenic Vein Thrombosis Impact
  • TIPS: no direct effect
  • RTO: may be less effective if SVT exists between TIPS and GV
  • RTO: absolute contraindication in most circumstances
Hepatocellular Carcinoma Impact
  • TIPS: may complicate placement if HCC involves hepatic parenchyma
  • RTO: unclear impact on portal HTN and EV/ascites risk
Intervention Comparison Summary (Table 2)
Control of Acute Bleeding
  • ECI: high efficacy
  • TIPS ± Direct Embolization: high efficacy
  • RTO (BRTO, CARTO, PARTO): high efficacy in presence of portos-systemic shunt
Rebleeding Risk
  • ECI: moderate
  • TIPS ± Direct Embolization: low
  • RTO (BRTO, CARTO, PARTO): lowest
Effect on MELD Score
  • ECI: no significant change
  • TIPS ± Direct Embolization: may worsen initially
  • RTO (BRTO, CARTO, PARTO): typically unchanged; longer-term can improve
Effect on Portal Pressure
  • ECI: no effect
  • TIPS ± Direct Embolization: decreases portal pressure
  • RTO (BRTO, CARTO, PARTO): increases portal pressure
Ascites
  • ECI: no effect
  • TIPS ± Direct Embolization: improves
  • RTO (BRTO, CARTO, PARTO): worsens
Hepatic Encephalopathy
  • ECI: no effect
  • TIPS ± Direct Embolization: worsens
  • RTO (BRTO, CARTO, PARTO): improves
Esophageal Varices
  • ECI: no effect
  • TIPS ± Direct Embolization: improves
  • RTO (BRTO, CARTO, PARTO): worsens
Risk of Distal Thromboembolism
  • ECI: 0.7-2%
  • TIPS ± Direct Embolization: <1%
  • RTO (BRTO, CARTO, PARTO): low
Risk of Portal-Splenic-Mesenteric Venous Thrombosis
  • ECI: none reported
  • TIPS ± Direct Embolization: none reported
  • RTO (BRTO, CARTO, PARTO): low
Technical Complexity
  • ECI: moderate/high (EUS guidance requires special expertise)
  • TIPS ± Direct Embolization: high (requires IR expertise)
  • RTO (BRTO, CARTO, PARTO): high (requires IR expertise)
Gastric Variceal Recurrence
  • ECI: low (dependent on adequate follow-up)
  • TIPS ± Direct Embolization: low
  • RTO (BRTO, CARTO, PARTO): lowest (<5%)
Follow-Up Management
GV Resolution Monitoring
  • Endoscopic and imaging follow-up required after definitive therapy
  • Ensures complete resolution without complications
Glue Cast Evolution After ECI
  • Evacuation of glue cast occurs after ECI
  • May take months, in rare cases years
  • Mucosal oozing may occur at glue cast site
Complications of Endovascular Therapies
  • Rare but possible
  • Consultation with interventional radiologists and/or endoscopists may be necessary
Secondary Prophylaxis - Lesser Curve GV
  • Beta blockers recommended (similar to EV bleeding)
  • Example: carvedilol
Secondary Prophylaxis - Cardiofundal GV
  • Beta blockers --> ineffective for cardiofundal GV (study proven)
  • Not contraindicated if needed for alternative reasons
RTO Procedure - Specific Follow-Up
  • Increases portal pressures because closes down portosystemic shunt
  • Recommend EGD within 4 weeks of RTO procedure
  • Re-assess changes to esophageal varices
Pre-existing Large EV Before RTO
  • If large (non-bleeding) EV present before RTO --> initiate primary prophylaxis for EV bleeding
  • Per standard guidelines
Primary Prophylaxis Choice After RTO
  • No definitive data supporting band ligation over beta blockade in this specific scenario
Algorithm Summary (Figure 6)
Initial Presentation
  • Suspected portal hypertensive bleeding
  • Clear fundus completely first
Acute Management Options

1. ECI --> preferred for temporizing but often unavailable due to expertise

2. Balloon tamponade --> generally safest acute intervention, can be placed endoscopically

3. Clip/thrombin/band ligation/ethanolamine --> reported but short/long-term durability lacking

  • Band ligation and ethanolamine injection will cause severe ulceration and rebleeding if definitive therapy not applied within 12-24 hours
Post-Stabilization Steps

1. Portal venous contrasted imaging with CT or MRI (CT generally better for visualizing vasculature)

2. Echocardiography preferred before TIPS placement

3. Multi-disciplinary discussion for treatment planning

Definitive Therapy Options
  • Endoscopic: ECI under direct endoscopy or with EUS guidance, ± metallic coils
  • Endovascular: TIPS placement (with direct embolization if no shunt) or RTO variants
Follow-Up Requirements
  • Endoscopic follow-up after endovascular therapy:
  • Ensure complete GV treatment (EUS with doppler can aid assessment)
  • Ensure no worsening of esophageal varices (RTO procedures only)
  • Endoscopic follow-up after endoscopic therapy: retreat with ECI as needed
Vascular Imaging Follow-Up
  • Ensure occlusion of gastric varix complex
  • Ensure occlusion of outflow tract (RTO procedures)
  • Ensure patency of TIPS shunt (TIPS procedures)
  • Ensure no vascular thromboses requiring intervention
Key Clinical Pearls
Why Cardiofundal GV Are Higher Risk
  • Lower prevalence than lesser curve GV
  • Highest risk of bleeding among GV types
  • More severe bleeding when it occurs
Importance of Fundus Clearance
  • Most common GV locations: posterior wall and greater curvature
  • Blood obscuring these areas leads to missed diagnosis
  • Treating EV first without clearing fundus can lead to inappropriate treatment
When ECI Not Available
  • Balloon tamponade is safest option for acute control
  • Transfer to center with ECI capability if definitive therapy needed
RTO Considerations
  • Requires patent portal-splenic vasculature (cannot have occlusive PVT)
  • Will worsen existing portal/splenic vein thromboses
  • Increases portal pressure --> monitor for EV worsening
TIPS Considerations
  • May not be adequate if SVT exists between TIPS and GV
  • Can worsen ascites initially
  • Increases hepatic encephalopathy risk
Conclusion
  • Further investigation with randomized controlled data required for ideal approach to each unique GV presentation
  • Algorithm provided offers safe and sound method until better treatment algorithms defined
  • Multi-disciplinary approach essential for optimal outcomes
